interactions.summary: Jiang explains memory's malleability and imagination's expansiveness, then replaces passive psychology with a Kant-Hegel model where humans actively create reality through space, time, and Geist.

He makes the model mythic: consciousness extends through infinite dimensions, memories enter the Geist, powerful memories become gods, God names the unwritten laws of the universe, and each person is a hologram of the conscious universe.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture with some ASR-damaged sentences around seg-0022.

claims.claim: Memories change as a person develops; the same event can be reinterpreted later and produce a different emotional reaction.
Jiang presents Kant as saying humans are not passive absorbers of experience but active participants who create reality through the forms of space and time.
He presents Hegel's Geist as the spirit or shared force that gives space and time, responds to human action, and lets multiple people inhabit the same reality.
Powerful memories stored in the Geist can give rise to new consciousnesses such as gods; older gods name deeper forces like honor, justice, fate, and destiny.
Each human being is a hologram of the universe, so a single act can reverberate through the entire conscious universe.

signature_moments.moment: The mind stops being a camera and becomes a world-maker.
signature_moments.source_phrase: we are active participants in reality, meaning that we create reality
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the metaphysical turn that lets Jiang read Homer as more than psychology.

signature_moments.moment: A person is not a small private self but a hologram of the entire conscious universe.
signature_moments.source_phrase: we are a hologram of the universe
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It makes individual action cosmically consequential inside Jiang's model.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's Hegelian name for the shared spirit or conscious force that responds to us and structures common reality.
The universe as a living field of memory, spirit, dialogue, and reciprocal influence rather than inert matter.
Defined as the immutable and unwritten laws of the universe, though this segment contains substantial ASR damage after the definition.

chronology_notes.note: This 2026-01-28 lecture uses Kant and Hegel to connect Great Books reading with Jiang's broader conscious-universe metaphysics.

uncertainty_notes.note: The ASR is badly damaged around the explanation after 'God as immutable and unwritten laws'; only the stable definitions and transitions should be used.
